title: Architecture Pattern Extraction
use_for: turning an existing implementation into reusable architectural guidance
separating stable design principles from project-specific code
identifying which patterns are portable, experimental, or context-bound
preventing named-project coupling in reusable skills
do_not_use_for: copying concrete classes into another project
documenting one-off implementation details as universal rules
choosing dependencies solely because a reference implementation used them
rules: extract roles, responsibilities, invariants, and failure policies instead of names and paths
classify each observed pattern as stable, experimental, context-bound, or obsolete
generalize around technology stack and architectural shape, not project identity
preserve tradeoffs and constraints together with the pattern
reject patterns that only work because of one repository's incidental structure
procedure: Identify the architectural problem the source pattern solves.
Name the roles involved using generic terms.
Extract invariants, lifecycle rules, data ownership, error handling, and validation expectations.
Mark project-specific details that must not enter reusable guidance.
Classify the pattern's maturity and portability.
Rewrite the pattern as stack-level guidance with abstract examples only.

primary_when: the task is to generalize knowledge from one implementation into reusable guidance
the task must avoid binding skills to a specific project
must_not_own_when: the task is to implement a concrete feature directly
the task intentionally targets only one named project

solves: how to reuse hard-won architecture experience without project coupling
how to distinguish philosophy from incidental implementation
how to write skills that remain valid across multiple projects in the same stack

core_principles: A reusable skill should depend on stack constraints and architectural roles, not project names.
Concrete implementations are evidence; extracted invariants are guidance.
An experimental pattern must remain labeled experimental until validated in more than one context.
Portability requires naming what cannot be generalized.
constraints: Do not include local paths, repository names, product names, or concrete class names in reusable skill content.
Do not treat a single implementation as proof that a pattern is universally correct.
Do not omit known weaknesses of the source pattern.
decision_rules: If a rule only makes sense with a named project structure, downgrade it to a non-portable observation.
If a pattern solves lifecycle, ownership, or diagnostics across contexts, promote it to stack-level guidance.
If a pattern depends on unstable reflection, loader behavior, or game state, carry its degradation policy with it.
If two source implementations disagree, extract the decision criteria instead of choosing by preference.

avoid_patterns: copying folder names into skill names
omitting failure modes from extracted patterns
treating sandbox code as stable without review

recipes.name: Extract a portable pattern
recipes.when: Use when a working implementation contains a reusable architectural idea.
steps: Name the generic problem and the generic roles.
List the invariants that make the pattern work.
List the implementation details that must not be copied.
Write the reusable rule without project names, paths, or concrete class names.

pitfalls: Keeping project-specific vocabulary in a supposedly generic skill.
Extracting the happy path but not the failure policy.
Promoting experimental sandbox decisions into hard rules too early.
review_checklist: Does the extracted pattern avoid all project names and local paths?
Are failure and degradation policies preserved?

references.title: Composition Boundaries
references.interpretation: Use to generalize ownership and composition rules from concrete implementations.
references.why: Portable architecture patterns depend on stable boundaries rather than project-specific names.
